full_text_rationale: Full text describes LLMA-Mem as a lifelong memory framework for multi-agent systems that distills procedural memories from episodic and transactive experience, supports cross-task reuse, and is evaluated on MultiAgentBench coding tasks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 012; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Shanglin Wu, Yuyang Luo, Yueqing Liang, Kaiwen Shi, Yanfang Ye, Ali Payani, Kai Shu
titulo: Scaling Teams or Scaling Time? Memory Enabled Lifelong Learning in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
formato_armazenamento: LLMA-Mem maintains episodic memory with task trajectories and outcomes, procedural memory with generalized strategies and success/failure counts, and transactive memory with agent profiles, team patterns, specialization, collaboration history, and success-rate estimates under local, shared, or hybrid topologies.
framework_agente: LLMA-Mem lifelong multi-agent memory framework integrated with MultiAgentBench collaborative environments, graph-based coordination, relevance-importance retrieval, episodic update, transactive update, and periodic procedural consolidation
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; lifelong sequence evaluation; model-family comparison; no-memory and memory-baseline comparison; cumulative moving average analysis; token-usage comparison; team-size scaling study; topology and consolidation-interval ablations
benchmarks_utilizados: MultiAgentBench collaborative coding, research, and database environments with 100 tasks per environment
metricas_desempenho: Task Score, Communication Score, Average of AS, cumulative moving average, cumulative memory advantage, average token usage per task
metricas_cl: Episodic-to-procedural consolidation every N tasks, success/failure counts for procedures, local/shared/hybrid memory topology comparison, team-size scaling from 1 to 7 agents, token-efficiency analysis over task sequences
baseline_comparado: No memory, MARBLE memory-enabled MultiAgentBench baseline, A-Mem dynamic memory framework, LLMA-Mem topology variants, LLMA-Mem consolidation-interval variants
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: The default setup retrieves top-3 memory items with maximum memory context 5 and consolidation interval N=5; memories persist as JSON files across tasks in the same sequential run.
custo_por_tarefa: Average token usage per task is compared across memory baselines; LLMA-Mem reduces token usage relative to MARBLE and A-Mem by 9.4% to 71.7% depending on model and environment.
reducao_tokens: Token reductions include 42.2% for Claude-Sonnet-4.5 on coding, 47.5% for DeepSeek-V3.2 on coding, 71.7% for Qwen3-32B-Instruct on research, 44.2% for Qwen3-32B-Instruct on database, 53.2% for Qwen3-next-80B on coding, 9.4% for DeepSeek-V3.2 on database, and 14.5% for Qwen3-next-80B on database.
resultado_principal: LLMA-Mem consistently improves Average of AS over the no-memory baseline in most model-environment pairs, with reported gains such as +5.92 for DeepSeek-V3.2 on research and +3.19 for Qwen3-32B-Instruct on database.
desempenho_vs_baseline: In the coding topology ablation with Qwen3-32B-Instruct, local memory obtains TS 49.68, CS 46.69, and AAS 49.43 versus shared 46.79/45.96/48.62 and hybrid 46.52/40.32/46.92.
custo_vs_baseline: LLMA-Mem lowers average token usage relative to MARBLE and A-Mem while improving long-horizon performance, showing that gains are not obtained by simply spending more context tokens.
limitacoes: The Qwen3-next-80B research setting shows reduced AAS despite higher task score, larger teams can incur coordination overhead and non-monotonic performance, and the study does not directly measure memory redundancy, staleness, or retrieval robustness.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on MultiAgentBench collaborative tasks, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 evaluation scoring, Amazon Bedrock inference settings, Titan embedding retrieval, chosen consolidation intervals, and whether coding/research/database environments represent broader multi-agent software work.
